behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is backoff math/state progression in one retry-policy function/object (MOCK-MISS: no cross-module handoff required). REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP are absent. Rejected Integration because behavior text is strictly delay calculation + cap, which can fail without wiring other modules.
behaviors.test_description: Call the backoff calculator (or retry-policy object) with consecutive failure counts and assert delays follow 2^n growth and clamp at configured max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at a module boundary: HTTP response classification (e.g., 429/headers) must feed retry-delay computation (MOCK-MISS yes). In-process fakes are sufficient; no real OS/network/subprocess required (REAL-INFRA no). No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry module with a stub transport that returns a rate-limit response (status/header variants) and assert retry decision=true plus computed wait matches policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Requires interaction of timer/scheduler, retry orchestrator, and request transport; unit-only logic would miss resend/handoff bugs (MOCK-MISS yes). Real network is not required because a stub transport plus controllable clock reproduces failure mode (REAL-INFRA no).
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock + stub transport: first response rate-limited, second response success. Assert original request payload is resent after expected delay and caller receives final success without manual retry handling.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on retry counter exhaustion plus error mapping at orchestration boundary, not just a standalone constructor check (MOCK-MISS yes). No real infra needed; deterministic stub failures can drive exhaustion path. Higher levels are disqualified because behavior text is about typed error after retry count, not real HTTP/network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries N, have stub transport always return rate-limit, run `call()`, and assert rejection is `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata (attempts/wait info) instead of generic error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is explicitly cross-module chain behavior (retry + backoff + quota tracker), so unit is too low (MOCK-MISS yes). Still in-process: controllable clock and stub transport can detect non-termination without real OS/network (REAL-INFRA no). No AI/agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run `call()` with persistent rate-limit responses under fake time advancement; assert total attempts and total scheduled waits are bounded and promise settles with expected terminal error instead of hanging/retrying forever.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure occurs in shared-state handoff across concurrent callers and quota manager module (MOCK-MISS yes). Behavior text explicitly scopes to same process, so no real distributed/OS boundary is required (REAL-INFRA no).
behaviors.test_description: Start multiple concurrent `call()` invocations against one client instance with a deterministic stub API/quota signals; assert aggregate consumption is enforced globally (not per-caller) and excess calls are delayed/failed according to shared bucket state.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering bugs arise from queue + scheduler + dispatcher interaction under async contention, beyond single-function logic (MOCK-MISS yes). Real network is unnecessary; stub transport and deterministic scheduling can expose reorder defects. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue several calls while quota blocks dispatch, then release capacity stepwise; assert send order matches submission order (FIFO) by inspecting dispatch timestamps/sequence IDs from the stub transport.
